Press enter or click to view image in full size

3 min read
Just now

--

As your Laravel application grows, you’ll often find yourself writing multiple actions inside a single controller.
For example, when a user registers, your application might need to:

• Create the user

• Send a welcome email

• Create a profile

• Log the activity

• Notify the administrator
Putting all of this logic inside one controller quickly makes your code difficult to read, maintain, and test.
Laravel solves this problem with Events and Listeners.

What Are Events?
An Event represents something that has happened in your application.
Examples include:

• UserRegistered

• OrderPlaced

• PaymentCompleted

• ProductCreated

• PasswordReset
The event itself doesn’t perform any action. It simply announces that something has happened.

What Are Listeners?
A Listener waits for a specific event and performs one task when that event occurs.
One event can have multiple listeners.
For example:
UserRegistered
│
├── SendWelcomeEmail
├── CreateUserProfile
├── LogUserActivity
└── NotifyAdminThis keeps every class focused on a single responsibility.

Creating an Event
Generate an event using Artisan.
php artisan make:event UserRegisteredLaravel creates:
app/Events/UserRegistered.phpExample:
<?phpnamespace App\Events;
use App\Models\User;
use Illuminate\Foundation\Events\Dispatchable;
use Illuminate\Queue\SerializesModels;
class UserRegistered
{
use Dispatchable, SerializesModels;
public function __construct(
public User $user
) {}
}
The event simply carries the user data.

Creating a Listener
Generate a listener.
php artisan make:listener SendWelcomeEmail --event=UserRegisteredLaravel creates:
app/Listeners/SendWelcomeEmail.phpExample:
<?phpnamespace App\Listeners;
use App\Events\UserRegistered;
use Illuminate\Support\Facades\Mail;
use App\Mail\WelcomeMail;
class SendWelcomeEmail
{
public function handle(UserRegistered $event): void
{
Mail::to($event->user->email)
->send(new WelcomeMail($event->user));
}
}
Notice how the listener has only one responsibility.

Dispatching the Event
After creating the user:
use App\Events\UserRegistered;$user = User::create($request->validated());
UserRegistered::dispatch($user);
That’s all.
Laravel automatically executes every listener attached to that event.

Multiple Listeners
The same event can trigger multiple independent actions.
For example:
UserRegistered→ SendWelcomeEmail
→ CreateProfile
→ GiveWelcomeBonus
→ NotifyAdmin
→ LogActivity
Instead of placing hundreds of lines inside one controller, every action lives in its own class.

Real-World Use Cases
Events and Listeners are commonly used for:

• Sending emails

• Sending notifications

• Logging activities

• Updating inventory

• Creating audit logs

• Awarding reward points

• Processing payments

• Synchronizing data with third-party services

Why Use Events & Listeners?
Using Events and Listeners offers several advantages:

• Cleaner controllers

• Better separation of concerns

• Easier testing

• Reusable business logic

• Improved project structure

• Better scalability
As your application grows, these benefits become even more valuable.

Best Practices
Keep listeners focused on a single task.
If a listener performs heavy work such as sending emails or generating reports, implement the ShouldQueue interface so the task runs in the background.
Avoid placing business logic inside the event itself.
The event should only describe what happened, while the listener decides what to do.

Final Thoughts
Events and Listeners are one of Laravel’s most powerful architectural features.
They allow you to build applications that are modular, maintainable, and easy to extend without constantly modifying existing code.
Whenever you notice a controller performing several unrelated tasks after a single action, it’s usually a sign that those responsibilities should be moved into Events and Listeners.
